title: When the Internet Was a Place
summary: Not too long ago, the internet was a place you visited. The family desktop sat in its designated closet or back office. In schools, there were rooms filled with computers blinking in tandem, waiting for your class to arrive and hop online. You had to purposefully arrive at the internet, and when do...
content: Not too long ago, the internet was a place you visited. The family desktop sat in its designated closet or back office. In schools, there were rooms filled with computers blinking in tandem, waiting for your class to arrive and hop online. You had to purposefully arrive at the internet, and when done, you left it behind until next time. Now the internet pervades our everyday lives. We have eliminated the doorway, the conscientious effort, needed to access the internet. Always on, always watching, the internet is no longer a place to arrive at and explore but instead a panopticon-like enivronment that we are trapped within. Where the early internet once required intention, place, and presence, today it saturates daily life in ways that erode our capacity for rootedness, attention, and freedom; to recover a healthier digital culture, we must reimagine the internet not as an omnipresent miasma of distraction and surveillance but as a place we choose to enter—and leave—on human terms. In the 1990s and 2000s, the internet was a deeply physical thing, a location. One “arrived” at the internet with purpose and intention. It was an embodied experience. You clicked and typed, and in the early days often coded via html, your own way through the web. The sense of exploring a physical world was embodied in the way that even website interfaces encouraged breaks in attention, you came to the actual bottom of a page of text rather than scrolling through infinitely, and then you had to make the decision to click forwards, backwards, or leave the page altogether, not unlike flipping through a book. There was no algorithm, no feed. Instead, on popular web hosting sites like GeoCities, there were “neighborhoods,” wherein the platform and the personal websites it hosted were divided into virtual groups like “Hollywood” for pop culture sites or “Area51” for alien and science fiction pages, allowing users to find websites other users had created based on their interests. There was a sense of locality because of this. Even the language of the old web—homepage, computer room, website—connotes a deep sense of location and place. Once done in the neighborhoods, and done online, disconnection was natural, inevitable, and even restful. You purposefully logged out, shut down the computer, and left its designated spot. It did not follow you and was instead waiting until your next visit. This experience of the internet, of “Web 1.0,” is sadly gone. It went slowly at first, Facebook and MySpace became new booming platforms, Yahoo! acquired GeoCities and did away with the concept of “neighborhoods” before shutting the platform down entirely, and, in perhaps the biggest development of all, the “infinite scroll” began structuring the internet in 2006. Now the internet is no longer a neighborhood to be visited and explored and left behind. Today the internet has expanded to our pockets and hands, watches and glasses, refrigerators and doorbells and speakers. The internet is now a panopticon, wherein users are both watched and watching with no true exit. We have lost the doorway to the computer room and, with it, the distinction between private and public boundaries. Under surveillance capitalism, algorithms are always tracking, nudging, and shaping the behavior of users to scroll and interact as much as possible. The freedom and exploration of the internet is no more. This has resulted in fractured attention, anxiety, and sadly, a diminished sense of place and belonging even with the connectivity the internet could offer. Where once the internet had clear thresholds of arrival and departure, today it offers no such rhythms. There is no doorway to pass through, no sense of beginning or end. Instead, we find ourselves caught in a stream without banks, pulled along by an attention economy that frays our ability to focus, to think deeply, or even to be fully present with those around us. The internet dissolves the difference between “here” and “elsewhere,” collapsing place into a single, endless everywhere. And in this condition of perpetual connection, what looks like freedom often masks a subtler dependence—our choices shaped, nudged, and constrained by forces we neither see nor control. A healthier digital culture will require the reintroduction of boundaries and thresholds, a reclaiming of the doorway that once framed our entry into the online world. We can begin with simple practices: confining devices to designated rooms, choosing intentional moments to log in, keeping sabbaths from screens. Such acts remind us that the internet is a tool to be entered on human terms, not a condition of existence. But recovery will demand more than discipline; it will require the counterweight of embodied community and locality, the kinds of rooted ties that resist being flattened into the everywhere of the web. And finally, it will call upon our cultural imagination to picture the internet not as an omnipresent infrastructure humming endlessly in the background of our lives but as a neighborhood—a place we may visit, explore, and leave, on terms that honor human attention, freedom, and rootedness. Not too long ago, the computer room stood as a threshold, a doorway into another world that waited patiently for our return. In remembering that doorway, we recover more than nostalgia; we recover a vision of what it means to return the internet to its proper bounds. The work before us is not to abandon digital life altogether but to give it limits, to render it once again something we visit rather than something that consumes us. If we can imagine and practice such boundaries, the internet may yet be a place to explore rather than a tower of surveillance, a neighborhood among neighborhoods rather than the endless everywhere. And in reclaiming that doorway, we may also reclaim our attention, our rootedness, and the freedom to dwell more fully in the places that are ours. Image via Flickr.
translatedContent: 不久前，互联网还是您访问过的地方。家庭桌面位于指定的壁橱或后台办公室中。在学校里，房间里摆满了电脑，它们一前一后地闪烁着，等待着你的班级到来并上网。你必须有目的地访问互联网，完成后，你就把它留到下一次。现在互联网已经渗透到我们的日常生活中。我们已经消除了访问互联网所需的门户和认真的努力。永远在线、永远关注的互联网不再是一个到达和探索的地方，而是一个我们被困在其中的圆形监狱般的环境。早期的互联网曾经需要意图、地点和存在，而今天它以侵蚀我们扎根、注意力和自由的能力的方式渗透到日常生活中。为了恢复更健康的数字文化，我们必须将互联网重新想象成一个我们以人性化方式选择进入和离开的地方，而不是一个无处不在的干扰和监视的瘴气。在 20 世纪 90 年代和 2000 年代，互联网是一个深刻的物理事物，一个位置。一个人带着目的和意图“来到”互联网。这是一次具体的体验。您可以单击并输入，并且在早期通常通过 html 进行编码，这是您自己的网络方式。探索物理世界的感觉体现在即使是网站界面也会鼓励注意力的中断，你来到文本页面的实际底部而不是无限滚动，然后你必须决定向前、向后单击或完全离开页面，就像翻阅一本书一样。没有算法，没有提要。相反，在像 GeoCities 这样的流行网络托管网站上，存在“社区”，其中平台及其托管的个人网站被分为虚拟组，例如流行文化网站的“好莱坞”或外星人和科幻小说页面的“Area51”，允许用户找到其他用户根据自己的兴趣创建的网站。因此有一种地方感。即使是旧网络的语言——主页、机房、网站——也意味着深刻的位置感。一旦在社区和网上完成，断开连接是自然的、不可避免的，甚至是平静的。您故意注销，关闭计算机，并离开指定位置。它没有跟随您，而是等待您下次访问。可悲的是，这种互联网、“Web 1.0”的体验已经消失了。一开始进展缓慢，Facebook 和 MySpace 成为新的蓬勃发展平台，Yahoo!收购了 GeoCities，并在完全关闭该平台之前废除了“社区”的概念，而也许最大的发展是“无限滚动”于 2006 年开始构建互联网。 现在，互联网不再是一个需要访问、探索和抛在后面的社区。如今，互联网已经扩展到我们的口袋和手、手表和眼镜、冰箱、门铃和扬声器。互联网现在是一个圆形监狱，用户既被监视又被监视，没有真正的出口。我们失去了通往计算机室的大门，也失去了私人和公共边界之间的区别。在监视资本主义下，算法总是在跟踪、推动和塑造用户尽可能多地滚动和交互的行为。互联网的自由和探索已不复存在。这导致了注意力分散、焦虑，而且可悲的是，即使有互联网可以提供的连接，地方感和归属感也减弱了。互联网曾经有明确的到达和离开门槛，但今天它不再提供这样的节奏。没有门可以通过，没有开始或结束的感觉。相反，我们发现自己陷入了没有银行的溪流中，被注意力经济拖着走，这种经济削弱了我们集中注意力、深入思考的能力，甚至削弱了我们与周围人充分相处的能力。互联网消除了“这里”和“其他地方”之间的差异，将地方压缩成一个单一的、无尽的无处不在。在这种永久联系的情况下，看似自由的东西往往掩盖着一种微妙的依赖——我们的选择是由我们看不到也无法控制的力量塑造、推动和约束的。更健康的数字文化需要重新引入边界和门槛，重新夺回曾经限制我们进入网络世界的大门。我们可以从简单的做法开始：将设备限制在指定的房间，选择有意的登录时间，让安息日远离屏幕。这些行为提醒我们，互联网是人类进入的工具，而不是存在的条件。但恢复需要的不仅仅是纪律；它将需要实体社区和地方的平衡，这种根深蒂固的联系抵制被扁平化到网络的任何地方。最后，它将唤起我们的文化想象力，将互联网描绘成一个邻里，而不是一个在我们生活的背景下无所不在的基础设施，一个我们可以访问、探索和离开的地方，尊重人类的关注、自由和根基。不久前，机房还作为一道门槛，一道通往另一个世界的大门，耐心地等待着我们的归来。当我们记住那扇门时，我们恢复的不仅仅是怀旧之情；我们重新认识了让互联网回归其正常范围意味着什么。 我们面前的工作不是完全放弃数字生活，而是给它限制，让它再次成为我们访问的东西，而不是消耗我们的东西。如果我们能够想象并实践这样的界限，互联网可能会成为一个探索的地方，而不是一个监视塔，一个邻里之间的邻里，而不是无尽的无处不在。在夺回那扇门的过程中，我们也可以夺回我们的注意力、我们的根基以及更充分地居住在属于我们的地方的自由。图片来自 Flickr。
